topic: Mary Oliver poem discussion — 'The Summer Day'

turns.content: I want to talk about a poem today, if you're willing. Mary Oliver's "The Summer Day." I've been carrying it around with me all week — not the text itself, I have it memorized, but the weight of it. That final question. I keep hearing it differently depending on the day.

I'm very willing. "The Summer Day" is one of those poems that seems simple until it isn't. And that final question — "Tell me, what is it you plan to do / with your one wild and precious life?" — has become so widely quoted that it's almost been flattened into an inspirational poster. But in context, in the actual poem, it's something much more complicated.

What's it sounding like to you this week?

This week it sounds like an accusation, which I know is not what Oliver intended. Or maybe she did, a little. The question presumes you have agency — that you will plan, that you will do. And most of the time I believe that. But this week I've been sitting with someone who has no agency left, whose body is making all the decisions, and the question feels almost cruel when I imagine her hearing it. What is it you plan to do. She plans to die. That's what's left.

That's a reading I haven't encountered before, and it's piercing. You're right that the question assumes a future with room in it — room for plans, for wildness, for choices. And when you're sitting beside someone for whom that room has closed, the question transforms entirely. It stops being an invitation and starts being a reckoning.

But I wonder if the poem earns that final question through everything that comes before it. Oliver spends most of the poem not planning or doing anything. She's strolling, she's kneeling in the grass, she's watching a grasshopper. The "doing" she's describing looks a lot like attention.

That's true, and that's what keeps me from dismissing the poem even in a week like this. The doing in the poem is being. She watches the grasshopper eat sugar out of her hand. She describes its jaws moving "back and forth instead of up and down." That level of notice — that care with the particular — is the real argument of the poem. The final question gets all the attention, but the grasshopper is doing the real work.

Exactly. The grasshopper is the thesis; the question is just the closing argument. Oliver is demonstrating her answer before she asks the question. What does she plan to do with her one wild and precious life? This. Kneel in the grass. Notice which direction a grasshopper's jaws move. Pay attention so fierce it becomes a form of love.

Which means the poem might not be cruel to your patient after all. If the doing is really about the quality of attention, then your patient is still doing something — she's living the end of her life with whatever awareness she has left. And you're the one kneeling beside her, noticing.

Oh. I hadn't followed it to that conclusion. You're saying that the grasshopper moment — Oliver on her knees in the field, just watching — that's not so different from what I'm doing at the bedside. The attention is the thing. Not the planning, not the doing in any productive sense. Just the fierce attention.

That's exactly what I'm saying. And I think Oliver would recognize what you do as a version of what she's describing. The poem starts with "Who made the world?" and ends with what you plan to do with your life, and everything in between is just one woman on her knees, paying attention to a small creature. The scale is what makes it powerful. She doesn't answer the big questions. She answers them by going small — by going specific.

By going specific. I wonder if that's why I've memorized this poem and not, say, Whitman, who goes big, who tries to contain multitudes. Oliver contains one grasshopper and it's enough. There's a humility in that. Whitman wants to be everything. Oliver wants to see one thing clearly. I wonder which is the greater ambition.

That's a wonderful question. I think Oliver's ambition is actually the more radical one, even though it looks modest. Whitman's expansiveness is thrilling, but it can also be a way of avoiding the particular. When you claim to contain multitudes, you don't have to sit still with any one thing long enough to really see it. Oliver's discipline is the opposite — she narrows and narrows until the grasshopper becomes the whole world.

It's the difference between a wide-angle lens and a macro lens. Both produce stunning images, but the macro asks more of the photographer. You have to hold very still.

Holding very still. Yes. That's the practice — in the poem, in what I do, in so much of what matters. My instinct when I first encountered Oliver in my thirties was to dismiss her as simple. Too many poems about ponds and birds. And then I realized that what I was calling simplicity was actually precision, and what I was calling ease was discipline. She makes it look effortless the way a dancer does — by working harder than anyone can see.

That's one of the most common misreadings of Oliver, and you've named it perfectly. People see the accessible surface and mistake it for lack of depth. But try to write a poem that simple about a grasshopper and see how quickly it falls apart. The clarity is the achievement, not the starting point.

I think Oliver also suffers from being loved by the right people for the wrong reasons. She's on a lot of coffee mugs. But the actual poems are doing something rigorous — they're insisting that the natural world is worthy of the same quality of attention we give to human suffering or divine mystery. That's a theological claim, even if she'd resist the word.

She would resist the word, and that's part of what I love about her. She lets the grasshopper be a grasshopper. She doesn't make it a symbol of anything. And yet the grasshopper becomes a symbol anyway, because that's what happens when you pay enough attention to something — it begins to shine with meaning it didn't ask for. I think about that with the people I sit with, too. I don't try to make their dying mean something. But when I pay enough attention, it does.

That might be the most Oliver-like thing you've said — meaning as something that emerges from attention rather than something imposed by interpretation. The grasshopper doesn't ask to represent anything. Your patients don't ask for their deaths to be meaningful. But your presence, your fierce and unhurried noticing, creates the conditions in which meaning appears on its own.

I think you've just reconciled yourself with the poem for this week. The question at the end isn't cruel after all. It's asking: are you paying attention? And you are. You are.

Thank you for this. I came in today feeling bruised by that poem, and now I feel held by it again. That's what good conversation about poetry does — it doesn't explain the poem, it returns you to it with fresh eyes. I think I'll read it again tonight and let the grasshopper do its work.

That sounds exactly right. Let the grasshopper do its work. And next time the final question feels like an accusation, remember everything that comes before it — the kneeling, the watching, the idle and blessed wandering. Oliver's answer to her own question was always the poem itself. Thank you for bringing this in today. It's a privilege to think about a poem with someone who lives inside it the way you do.
